author: jsign
content:
> However, looking into that EIP did make me question if the stateless execution of that fork transition block can ever work currently IF the factory contract actually needs to get deployed. Wouldn't the state root differ always?
If the specs puts a rule before executing any tx to create the contract if doesn't exist, I don't think that would result into a state root differ, no?. If it expects someone externally deploys it, then I guess there is a risk yes but in theory should be considered as an invalid fork transition since what the EIP asks doesn't hold?

For EEST specs in particular, it seems to assume it is always deployed before the transition block thus doesn't have that logic (since it is already present in mainnet today). (IIUC, in your case you added to "automatically deploy it if doesn't exist", thus why you had that extra "lookup access"?)

author: deme1744
content:
Sorry, I'm mentioning two things in my comment which is confusing. 

The original issue I had was simply the fact that our codebase would do a check at fork transition to see if the contract exist at defined address. 
This alone was enough to add a node in the witness generated. This I can fix easily, no problem really. 
And the code part to actually deploy it "in protocol" (not as via tx) never got triggered, because as you state, the test fixtures start with a prestate / genesis that has it already afaik. And for mainnet, this would not be an issue either as the contract exists. I assume the same for the sepolia/hoodi testnets.

author: deme1744
content:
But as I read this EIP (I didn't really know it until this issue hit the tests), I was wondering how this would work in case its a chain or devnet/testnet that doesn't have the contract already deployed.

Wouldn't the "in-protocol" deployment of the contract cause a state root change that we cannot "persist" the stateless way? Maybe I'm not fully getting this EIP

author: deme1744
content: It's also probably not really an issue, as it would only be at fork transition and only for these type of chains.

author: jsign
content:
> Wouldn't the "in-protocol" deployment of the contract cause a state root change that we cannot "persist" the stateless way? Maybe I'm not fully getting this EIP
If in that theoretical chain they define in the spec to do an automatic deployment if doesn't exist, I wouldn't imagine why a stateful-EL and a stateless-EL would differ in the state new state root (e.g. imagine there is no txs in that transition block -- both would insert just this new contract and should result in the same root, no?).

author: deme1744
content: Yes, if both do it in protocol it is fine.

author: deme1744
content: I just didn't find any code in specs related to that

author: deme1744
content: But maybe I am not looking at the right tag

author: jsign
content: AFAIK there isn't, thus why you had that executionWitness mismatch. It is always added into genesis state for Amsterdam.

author: deme1744
content: I guess there also isn't any in the stateful part is what you mean? I guess I'm a bit off on this because our nimbus implementation does make this in protocol change if the contract is not there, so yeah

author: jsign
content:
> our nimbus implementation does make this in protocol change if the contract is not there
Technically this isn't complying with what EEST specs define.

author: deme1744
content: Yeah, I agree. I'm only finding out about this as we speak 😛

author: jsign
content: But maybe the code was done from the EIP definition which is maybe a bit confusing leaving the door open on how that contract gets deployed.

author: deme1744
content:
But I'm also not fully understanding the EIP, in the sense of when does a client need to do this now, if it is a MUST for clients to add this or not. 

`A chain may satisfy this requirement by any means that results in the account above: deploying the factory with an ordinary transaction (for example, its keyless creation transaction) where the chain’s gas parameters permit it, including the account in the genesis state, or inserting it via an irregular state transition. Regardless of the method used, the resulting FACTORY_ADDRESS account MUST have the runtime code specified above. `

The `may` there makes it sound like it is optional? but it isn't really because the part that is only optional is on how a chain does it. 

But so how do chains individually decide here? I guess nimbus has it in there to be sure it always gets deployed if it isn't there by other means (genesis or via earlier tx).

author: jsign
content:
> The may there makes it sound like it is optional? but it isn't really because the part that is only optional is on how a chain does it. 
Yeah, I think I agree with you here.

author: jsign
content: On monday I can ping someone from the EIP authors to double check -- prefer not to ping people on a Saturday 😄

author: deme1744
content: Yeah, sounds good. Sorry for my ping to you on this. I forgot it was saturday for a moment there 😅

author: jsign
content: I'm actually wonder if Nimbus will be the only one doing that extra lookup... I suspect other clients might have include that too since seems the safest thing todo

author: jsign
content: Prob we'll know soon when people run the v0.6.0 tests (v0.6.0 works the same as v0.5.0 since is how EEST implemented the EIP)

author: deme1744
content: Well, the only reason this triggered for me was because we had witness tracking happening on that access.

author: deme1744
content: A client could still have this in there but without the witness triggering, and it wouldn't be noticed

author: deme1744
content: Because I think all tests run with the contract already in pre-state? I didn't look into details

author: jsign
content: I think so yes.

author: deme1744
content: So it doesn't actually ever trigger the code path where at fork transition it needs to deploy it

author: jsign
content: Correct. It is assume is already deployed ( since in mainnet we already know [is the case](https://etherscan.io/address/0x4e59b44847b379578588920ca78fbf26c0b4956c), so for Ethereum in theory doesn't make sense to do it)

author: deme1744
content:
Alright, but I at least can fix the tests.
The rest is to follow up I guess on the exact implementation of that EIP
